notes: I have treated the brief as one connected operating problem, not a set of separate finance tasks. The answer is deliberately practical: what I would do in the first 90 days, what I would not overpromise, and what I would need from leadership.
The first assumption is the only one that changes the sequencing. If the burn rate is materially different, I would recalibrate the urgency, but not the logic.
I am not criticising the team. I am saying the model is missing known operating facts. That is much easier to fix than random unpredictability.

This is the key interpretation. If the board remembers one slide, it should be this one. Data accuracy, collections, forecasting, the KPI question and the fixed-price shift are five breaks in one revenue-to-cash spine.

I would not try to fix everything at once. I would run workstreams in parallel, but the decision order is cash, truth, then discipline. Forecasting is not a phase; it is the output when collections behave and the data is trusted.
This is designed to be credible. It is about cadence, ownership and visibility, not buying software. Nothing on this slide needs a hire, a system or a budget.
The point is not to collect everything. The point is to stop treating aged receivables as one number and start managing them as decisions.
A dashboard does not create truth. Definitions create truth. The scorecard is only useful if every number has an owner and a contract. Cash numbers first, delivery second, commercial third.

I would not frame fixed-price as a finance objection. It is commercially attractive. But it needs guardrails or the downside lands in margin. A 30% deposit on a €300k deal is €90k in the bank before an hour is worked.

I would show support for the commercial ambition, but I would insist on sequencing. Fixed-price without trusted delivery economics is not strategy; it is risk transfer to the company. The gate is not a brake — it is what makes 40–50% fixed-price possible without making it fatal.

This slide makes the control practical. The value is not perfection. The value is finding the problem while there is still time to act. The tripwire does not stop estimates being wrong; it stops wrong estimates staying invisible.

This is the credibility slide. It shows ambition without overpromising. I commit to artefacts, not outcomes I do not control. I cannot promise that a client pays; I can promise the decision about that client is made and executed.
This makes the board part of the solution without asking for budget. That is powerful. Give me those three and by day 90 confidence stops being a word on a slide.

This appendix is useful if they push on the cash logic. It keeps the conversation focused on timing, runway and decision rights. This chart is not a problem solved; it is a countdown converted into a plan.
